title: The Mystery of the Missing Mascot

narrative.intro: The school mascot costume has vanished from the gym storage room — right before the big pep rally! Principal Reeves has called in the Logic Bureau (that's you) to investigate. You'll review interviews, texts, emails, and social media posts. But watch out: everyone's got a theory, and not all of them make sense...
narrative.outro: Thanks to your sharp eye for bad reasoning, the costume was found stuffed behind the bleachers. The real story? A student borrowed it for a TikTok and forgot to return it. Case closed, detective.

cases.briefing: Your first interview. Tommy saw something at lunch — but is his logic as solid as he thinks?
cases.speaker: Tommy Chen
claims: I was shooting hoops near the gym at lunch.
I saw Jake come out of the storage room around 12:10.
Everyone knows Jake is a troublemaker, so he obviously took the mascot costume.
The gym door locks automatically after that.
options: Tommy is using Jake's reputation instead of actual evidence that Jake took it.
Tommy didn't actually see Jake holding the costume.
cases.explanation: Tommy jumps from 'Jake has a bad reputation' to 'Jake did it.' But someone's character isn't proof they committed a specific act. This is called Ad Hominem — attacking the person instead of presenting real evidence.

cases.briefing: Mia sent these texts to the student council group chat. She's convinced she knows who did it...
cases.speaker: Mia Park
claims: OMG the mascot costume is gone!! I organized the pep rally so I know exactly who had access
Only the drama club and cheer squad had gym keys
I asked around and literally EVERY student thinks the drama club did it 💀
we should just search their lockers tbh
options: Just because lots of students suspect the drama club doesn't make them guilty.
Mia shouldn't have access to other students' lockers.
cases.explanation: Mia's evidence is 'everyone thinks they did it' — but popular opinion isn't proof! Lots of people believing something doesn't make it true. This is the Bandwagon fallacy — using crowd agreement as if it were evidence.

cases.briefing: Coach Davis fired off this email to the principal. He wants answers fast — maybe too fast.
cases.speaker: Coach Davis
claims: I've coached here for 15 years and nothing like this has ever happened.
The storage room lock wasn't broken, so someone with a key did this.
Either it was a student who stole a key, or we cancel the pep rally entirely.
Those are the only options I see. Please advise ASAP.
options: Coach is pretending there are only two options when other explanations exist.
The coach has no evidence that a key was stolen.
15 years of experience doesn't make the coach's theory correct.
cases.explanation: Coach Davis presents only two choices: stolen key or cancel everything. But there could be other explanations — maybe someone borrowed the costume with permission, maybe a teacher left the door open. This is a False Dilemma — pretending there are only two choices when more exist.

cases.briefing: Vince posted his 'investigation' online. His followers are eating it up, but is his logic solid?
cases.speaker: ViralVince
claims: ok so I just found out the mascot costume is MISSING 😱
the drama club had rehearsal yesterday and the costume was still there after
drama club rehearsal ended, and THEN the costume disappeared
so obviously rehearsal caused the costume to go missing lol. case closed 🔒
options: Just because the costume disappeared after rehearsal doesn't mean rehearsal caused it.
Vince doesn't have enough followers to be a reliable source.
The drama club should have locked up after rehearsal.
cases.explanation: Vince assumes the costume vanished BECAUSE of drama rehearsal just because it vanished AFTER. But timing isn't causation! Maybe someone else came in later. This is False Cause — assuming that because B happened after A, A must have caused B.

cases.briefing: Mr. Gonzalez left these sticky notes on Principal Reeves' desk with his observations.
cases.speaker: Mr. Gonzalez
claims: I clean the gym every evening after activities end
Yesterday I noticed muddy footprints leading toward the bleachers
Last month, a 5th grader tracked mud into the library and got in trouble
So a 5th grader DEFINITELY took the costume and hid it behind the bleachers
options: Mr. Gonzalez is using one past incident to blame all 5th graders for this.
Muddy footprints don't prove anyone took the costume.
The janitor should have reported the footprints immediately.
cases.explanation: One 5th grader tracked mud once, so Mr. Gonzalez concludes a 5th grader must be responsible this time too. That's like saying 'my neighbor's dog barked last week, so every noise must be a dog.' This is Hasty Generalization — jumping to a big conclusion from too little evidence.

cases.briefing: The vice principal sent this official memo. She has security footage — but where does her logic actually lead?
cases.speaker: VP Rodriguez
claims: We've reviewed the hallway security camera footage from 11:45 AM to 12:45 PM.
Three students were seen near the gym entrance during that window.
The real issue here is that our school doesn't have enough funding for proper security cameras inside the gym.
If we had interior cameras, this never would have happened. I'll be presenting a budget proposal to the school board next week.
options: The VP switches from 'who took the costume' to 'we need more budget' — a completely different topic.
Security cameras wouldn't necessarily prevent theft.
Three students near the gym isn't enough to identify the thief.
The VP should interview those three students first.
cases.explanation: VP Rodriguez starts with useful evidence (camera footage, three students) then pivots to school funding — a totally different issue! The budget has nothing to do with solving this case. This is a Red Herring — introducing an irrelevant topic to distract from the real question.
